dialogue.utterance: Good morning, I'm EMT Alex. What made you call 911 this morning?
I have trouble breathing. It started about an hour ago.
Can you keep your eyes open and follow my voice?
Yes, I can hear you.
Can you point to your left hand for me?
You appear A&Ox4.
Your airway is clear and maintainable.
Are you having any chest pain or discomfort?
No, I don't have chest pain.
Your pulse is regular, and we will proceed with more detailed measures soon.
Did you have any other symptoms besides shortness of breath?
No, only difficulty breathing.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take Digoxin, Atrovent, Lasix, Prednisone, and Pulmicort in the morning.
What chronic conditions do you have?
I have COPD, atrial fibrillation, and hypothyroidism.
When was the last time you had something to eat or drink?
I had breakfast and juice about 6:30 a.m.
When did the breathing difficulties first occur?
Around an hour ago.
Does it get worse during any particular activities or situations?
It gets worse when I feel hot.
How would you describe the feeling-tight, heavy, or difficult?
It's just a feeling of not taking a full breath.
Has it traveled to any other part of your body?
It hasn't spread anywhere else.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate the breathing difficulty now?
About a 5, but it's better now.
Has the difficulty improved, stayed the same, or worsened since it started?
It improved after the nebulizer treatment.
I'll now check your skin. Do you have any allergies?
No known allergies.
Your skin shows no signs of abnormal turgor or rash.
I don't notice any visible neck masses or swelling.
I saw no signs of obstruction or drainage when checking your nose and mouth.
I will now listen to your lungs for any abnormal noises.
Okay.
Your breath sounds are clear. You appear A&Ox4.
The EKG showed atrial fibrillation and signs of a possible old anterior infarct.
There were also minimal signs of left ventricular hypertrophy and a short QT interval noted on the EKG.
Because of your COPD and difficulty breathing, a DuoNeb treatment was initiated.
We performed a 4-lead ECG, and two 12-lead ECGs at different times.
Your blood pressure is 128 over 87. A few minutes ago, it was 117 over 78.
Your pulse rate started at 73 and increased to 87 later this hour.
Your breathing frequency is 16 per minute, it increased earlier to 20.
Your oxygen saturation is at 97%, which briefly dropped to 96% earlier.
I tried to check your blood sugar, but the device did not provide a result-possibly due to external factors or equipment limitations.
We're following protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress based on your EKG and history.
You remain stable and chose not to transport despite medical recommendation.
Your decision to refuse transport was documented and witnessed by a nurse.
Your vitals remain stable and consistent with those we first recorded shortly after arrival.
Your breathing is continuing to improve as noted from the nebulizer treatment.
Yes, I am breathing more easily now. I still rate the breathing difficulty at about a 5, but it is manageable.
The EKGs showed atrial fibrillation, old infarct changes, and mild signs of left ventricular hypertrophy as well as a short QT interval in some leads.
No new changes in condition or vitals occurred from our last review to now.
No further protocols are needed based on current condition and medical stability.
All EMS response was performed per your agreement and documentation of your AMA wishes here on-site.
The protocols were followed correctly in accordance with your situation and your refusal to transport was properly entered in our records as AMA with a legal witness of the resident nursing staff.
Our procedures here are complete, and care returned to the facility staff at your request.
Should any symptoms return or change, emergency services can be called again at any time per standard procedures.
All patient rights and informed refusal were upheld throughout the EMS involvement here at the home.
If needed, your current health condition, medications, and EKG results are on file and available for review by facility or hospital staff when necessary.
We left behind the nebulizer in active use and ensured the facility understands its continued use as part of treatment for your COPD stabilization.
The entire EMS encounter was documented clearly here, following all applicable protocols and legality regarding your choice to remain on-site.
Further examination of your skin confirmed no signs of injury, irritation, or any other physical issue consistent with signs of illness or distress outside COPD.
Your neck shows no signs of swelling, masses, or rigidity at the base of your skull consistent with neurological concern or injury.
Inside your nose and mouth, no obstructions, blockage, or signs of recent trauma or illness were seen during routine visual inspection.

Rechecking your breath sounds confirmed they remain clear and similar to initial assessment with no new adventitious sounds or equal expansion consistent with stable pulmonary status following nebulizer use.
Your abdomen appears distended but without pain or tenderness on percussion or palpation and with no signs of serious issue or complication currently at the moment.
Your limbs appear equal in size, color, and function-visually and consistent with no reported pain or mobility issues hindering your use of them at this time.

No symptoms, signs, or history of issues in the genital or urinary regions were observed today as part of a complete secondary assessment per EMT scope of practice guidelines provided in protocol 3-11.

Just to clarify, have you experienced any other new symptoms since the time of initial call from the facility staff or EMT arrival to the location of the incident this morning prior to our assessment and documentation here on-site today?
No, no new symptoms have appeared. Only the breathing difficulty since your arrival here and then the rest of the time at home prior to us speaking on-site this morning at approximately 7:00 a.m.

No additional interventions were performed beyond the DuoNeb treatment initiated based on standard protocol for COPD exacerbation with no signs of increased distress in this repeated reassessment round as well as supported by EKG readings and stable vitals encountered on arrival and following evaluation of response to treatment administered to date here on-site this morning.

All EMS equipment and materials were packed and transported out in full with no materials left behind per standard EMS protocol on departure this morning from the location due to resident choice and formal document of AMA refusal as you requested at arrival.

We ensured the storage and containment of all EMS-related tools and supplies safely and documented their transmittal back to队 vehicle this morning per EMS agency policy on departure from the location of this incident following protocol 3-11 instructions for COPD assessment and treatment accordingly.

Again, emergency services remain available in case any new issues arise, however, excellent cooperation and communication from you were seen among patient and EMT to ensure proper functioning of our EMS mission and integrity of medical care today at this house call site on this date of August 6, 2019 at [CITY] address redacted for review this morning between 7:10 a.m. and time of our departure here on-site later in the morning this day after full EMS response to resident call complies with all city, state, and facility regulations and policies during your breathing difficulty incident described here per EMT protocol.

We need you to understand, of course, if any concerns appear in the future, we can respond again or facility staff can call us again as appropriate for any change during or after this incident and related treatment this morning at AM refusal with nurse witness as described in our ambulance log and EPC redacted data provided to your home facility or support team following our actions here this morning as part of daily records updates via EMS computerized data system inputs by dispatch and field crews per normal emergency medical rounds practices in our area during our service area protocols standard operations procedures in accordance with article 3-11 medical department for asthma, COPD, exacerbation, reactive airway, or croup conditions as explained here with informed refusal for AMA patient determination remaining in full as to her procedures here on-site per your written documentation unsigned for legal but not medical deficiency recognition purposes per standard EMT field rules and written agency and health care facility agreements for the time of this incident registered in our EMS agency central dispatch logs as functioning effectively with full transparency per seam plan for this 85-year-old Cincinnati-Ohio native during sunrise emergency condition relating to heat-induced dyspnea of approximately 60 minutes in duration for medical follow-up: again, per AMA protocol and assisted by nurse aid and EMT with field EKG, DuoNeb, and normal vitals including low glucose definitive readings, mild AF Abn, Normal DPL and aged psoriasis notes confirmed at transport refusal here on-site.

Thank you for your cooperation and your willingness to communicate with us this morning through your breathing difficulties at your home situation. We remain at service should the need for additional help arise at any time in the future from your current licensed EMS squad with further United States standard in-home medical response capability as reported in spring annually here in our [CITY] EMS system.

Your stable condition and well-managed COPD with normal mental status, following nebulizer given, along with optional EKG review by nurse aid and included AMA procedures described, indicates good control and optimization following your early symptoms described to us this 2019 date at approximately 7 a.m. as recorded in the field report for this EMT patient with no new symptoms at time of departure and remaining aspirations in place by patient's own decisions supporting field outcome assessment here this morning.
